Credit Card Fraud Analysis

Exploratory analysis of transaction risk, card-not-present activity, and fraud concentration using a realistic synthetic dataset.

Python Pandas Matplotlib Jupyter Notebook

Project Overview

This project studies a realistic synthetic card-transaction dataset to identify the transaction patterns that are most strongly associated with fraud. The goal is to understand where fraud is concentrated and to recommend practical controls for prevention.

Business Problem

Why is fraud concentrated in a relatively small group of card-not-present, online, international, high-velocity transactions, and which transaction features should be prioritized to reduce fraud losses?

Project Statistics

Raw records50,500
Cleaned records50,000
Duplicate rows removed500
Missing cells in raw data7,284
Overall fraud rate3.12%
Average amount$38.06
Average credit limit$9,193.69
Average tenure47.38 months
Average age48.94 years
Years covered2021–2024
Channels4
Merchant categories12
Countries10
Card types4
High-risk share19.06%
High-risk fraud share52.72%

Key Findings

  • Overall fraud rate: 3.12%.
  • Online transactions show a fraud rate of 7.31% versus 1.19% for in-store purchases.
  • Card-not-present transactions show a fraud rate of 5.36%.
  • High-risk transactions account for 19.06% of all transactions but 52.72% of fraud cases.
  • Fraud rises sharply for high-velocity transactions and high-risk merchant categories.

Monthly Fraud Trend

Problem Identified

Fraud is concentrated in online, card-not-present, international transactions with high transaction velocity. Night-time activity and high-risk merchant categories such as travel, gaming, electronics, and luxury further increase exposure.

Root Cause Analysis

The strongest fraud drivers are transaction channel, card presence, international exposure, velocity, and merchant category. This means fraud prevention should focus on transaction context and behavioral patterns rather than only transaction amount.

Recommendations

  • Tighten controls for card-not-present and international transactions.
  • Use step-up authentication for high-risk online and mobile purchases.
  • Monitor velocity spikes and repeated attempts within short time windows.
  • Apply stricter rules for travel, gaming, electronics, and luxury merchants.
  • Increase alerts for night-time transactions and foreign card usage.

Conclusion

The analysis shows that fraud is not evenly distributed across the portfolio. Targeted monitoring and risk-based controls focused on online, card-not-present, international, and high-velocity transactions can significantly reduce fraud exposure.
